title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about the importance of play for adults. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Grown-Ups at Play
content.subtitle: An article exploring why adults need to play.
content.text:
Ask most adults when they last played — truly played, without agenda or outcome — and you will likely be met with an awkward pause. Play, it seems, is something we associate almost exclusively with childhood: a phase to be moved through and eventually outgrown. [31] Yet researchers studying human behaviour increasingly argue that this transition away from play is neither inevitable nor harmless, and that adults who neglect it do so at a psychological cost. [/31]

The question of what constitutes play is more complex than it first appears. Stuart Brown, a psychiatrist who has spent decades studying the subject, defines play not by the activity itself but by its qualities: it is purposeless in the immediate sense, intrinsically motivated, and undertaken for the pleasure it generates rather than any external reward. [32] By this definition, a person arranging flowers for relaxation is playing; a professional florist working to deadline is not — even if the activity looks identical from the outside. [/32] The distinction lies entirely in the inner state of the person engaged in it.

The benefits of adult play are well documented. Neurologists have found that playful activity stimulates the same neural pathways involved in creativity and problem-solving. Companies including Google and Pixar have redesigned their workplaces specifically to encourage unstructured, exploratory activity among employees, reporting improvements in innovation and staff wellbeing. [33] What these organisations discovered was that the brain, when freed from the pressure to produce, often produces its most interesting work. [/33]

Yet despite the evidence, many adults feel a deep resistance to permitting themselves play. The causes are partly cultural. In societies that prize productivity and measurable achievement, time spent in undirected activity can feel wasteful or even shameful. [34] One study found that adults asked to play freely in a laboratory setting reported feeling self-conscious and anxious — not because the activities were unpleasant, but because they had internalised the belief that enjoyment without purpose was somehow irresponsible. [/34]

There is also the problem of forgetting how to play. Children play spontaneously; adults must often relearn. Therapists who use play therapy with adults report that initial sessions are characterised by stiffness and discomfort, as clients struggle against years of conditioning. [35] Progress comes not when clients discover new activities, but when they reconnect with what they loved doing before the world told them to stop. [/35]

The argument for restoring play to adult life is ultimately not about leisure. It is about cognitive health, emotional resilience, and the quality of human relationships. [36] Play teaches us to tolerate uncertainty, to experiment without fear of failure, and to remain curious in the face of the unfamiliar — skills that are no less important at forty than they are at four. [/36] The adults who play, research suggests, are not those who have failed to grow up; they are those who have understood that growing up need not mean growing rigid.

questions.question: In the opening paragraph, the writer claims that adults who stop playing
options: A) are following a natural and unavoidable pattern of development.
B) suffer psychological consequences as a result.
C) are simply responding to the demands of adult life.
D) have been encouraged to do so by researchers.
questions.explanation: The text says adults who neglect play do so 'at a psychological cost', meaning they suffer negative psychological consequences.

questions.question: Stuart Brown's definition of play emphasises that
options: A) the activity must be creative and artistic.
B) it should take place outside of a working environment.
C) what matters is the person's motivation and inner experience, not the activity itself.
D) it produces a measurable improvement in mental health.
questions.explanation: The example of the florist shows that 'The distinction lies entirely in the inner state of the person', not the activity itself.

questions.question: The writer mentions companies like Google and Pixar to show that
options: A) large corporations are more progressive than smaller businesses.
B) encouraging unstructured activity can improve employee performance.
C) modern workplaces have become too focused on entertainment.
D) play is only beneficial when it takes place in a professional context.
questions.explanation: These companies redesigned workplaces to encourage play and reported 'improvements in innovation and staff wellbeing', demonstrating the professional benefits of play.

questions.question: The study mentioned in the fourth paragraph suggests that adults resist play because
options: A) they find playful activities genuinely unpleasant.
B) they have absorbed social messages that purposeless enjoyment is wrong.
C) laboratories are not appropriate environments for play.
D) they have forgotten what activities they enjoyed as children.
questions.explanation: Adults felt anxious 'because they had internalised the belief that enjoyment without purpose was somehow irresponsible' — a social message they had absorbed.

questions.question: According to therapists who use play therapy, adults make progress when they
options: A) are introduced to activities they have never tried before.
B) overcome their physical discomfort during sessions.
C) rediscover activities they enjoyed before societal pressure made them stop.
D) learn to play alongside other adults in a group setting.
questions.explanation: Progress comes 'when they reconnect with what they loved doing before the world told them to stop', meaning rediscovering childhood pleasures.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the writer argues that play develops skills that
options: A) are more important for children than for adults.
B) cannot be developed through any other means.
C) are equally relevant throughout a person's life.
D) only become apparent in professional situations.
questions.explanation: The writer says these skills 'are no less important at forty than they are at four', meaning they are equally important at all ages.
